sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 73, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Level 2 has a vertical divider, dark gray L-shape (left panel), yellow L-shape (left panel, below), black L-shape with white dots (right panel) - SPACE makes the yellow shape grow from 61 to 72 pixels (matching dark gray shape's size) and changes dot color from sky blue to white - After SPACE, DOWN now moves the dark gray shape by 3 rows - LEFT/RIGHT moves the dark gray shape by 6 cols, but the direction seems inconsistent - need to re-verify - Current: dark gray at rows 42-53, cols 21-35; yellow at rows 42-53, cols 3-17 - Goal: align dark gray shape with yellow shape (same position)
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 47 of about 50 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE changed the board but didn't complete the level. Let me check what changed. Looking at the image, the dots on the black shape changed from white to sky blue. So pressing SPACE again toggled the dot color back.
Let me check the segmentation to see what changed.
Actually, looking at the image, the dots on the black shape appear to be sky blue now (lighter blue), not white. So SPACE toggles the dot color between white and sky blue.
I'm running out of actions (47 of 50). Let me think about what the goal could be.
Maybe the goal is to align the dark gray shape with the yellow shape. The yellow shape is at rows 42-53, cols 3-17. The dark gray shape is at rows 39-50, cols 12-26.
To match the yellow shape, I need to move the dark gray shape to rows 42-53, cols 3-17. But the shape can't move further left than cols 12-26.
Wait, maybe I need to move the shape to a different vertical position where it can move further left. Let me check if the shape can move left at different vertical positions.
Actually, I already tested this. At the top position (rows 18-29), RIGHT moves the shape left by 3 cols. At the bottom position (rows 42-53), RIGHT moves the shape left by 3 cols. But the shape is blocked at cols 12-26.
Hmm, maybe the goal is to move the dark gray shape to overlap with the yellow shape in a specific way. The current overlap is at rows 42-50, cols 12-17.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the problem. The dark gray shape is at rows 39-50, cols 12-26. The yellow shape is at rows 42-53, cols 3-17. They overlap at rows 42-50, cols 12-17.
Maybe the goal is to have the dark gray shape completely cover the yellow shape, or vice versa. But the shapes have the same size (72 pixels), so they can't completely cover each other unless they're in the same position.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe the goal is to move the dark gray shape to the position of the black shape (on the right panel). But the dark gray shape is on the left panel, and I don't know if it can cross the divider.
Actually, let me try moving the dark gray shape to the right panel by moving it right (which moves it left, based on the reversed controls). Wait, that doesn't make sense.
Let me try moving the shape right (which moves it left) and see if it can cross the divider.
Actually, the shape is at cols 12-26. The divider is at cols 36-38. The shape is far from the divider, so moving it right (which moves it left) would move it further away from the divider.
To move the shape towards the divider, I need to move it left (which moves it right, based on the reversed controls). But at the current position (rows 39-50), LEFT moves the shape right by... let me check.
Actually, I haven't tested LEFT at rows 39-50. Let me test it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after SPACE
# First check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['pixels'] >= 50 and n['color'] in ['c', 'Y', 'B']:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"{n['color']}: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check the dots on the black shape
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'w' or n['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Dot {n['color']}: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Try LEFT at current position (rows 39-50)
action(['LEFT'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg2:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Dark gray after LEFT: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
c: row 39-50, col 12-26, pixels=72
B: row 39-50, col 48-62, pixels=64
Y: row 42-53, col 3-17, pixels=72
B: row 63-63, col 0-62, pixels=63
Dot S: row 0-62, col 36-38, pixels=168
Dark gray after LEFT: row 39-50, col 6-20
